title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices Fall WoW Along with Significant Increase in Type 1 Transfer Volumes
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(8th â€“ 14th September 2025)
  • A total of 442,613 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a massive rise of 2x week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a rise of 18.32% WoW as 758,894 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $55.52/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 1.93% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers declined by 1.53% in price, hitting $55.98/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined by 1.80% WoW; on 16th September, the spot price stood at $51.63/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $53.75/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 1.38% WoW. Both D4 and D6 prices fell by 3.96% WoW, while D5 price fell by 4.95% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

In California, sources indicated that legislators were in talks last week about a potential agreement to cover maintenance costs at Valero Energyâ€™s Benicia Refinery. The discussions aim to prevent the San Francisco-area fuel plant from shutting down and ensure continued operations at Benicia. In a recent announcement by Governor Gavin Newsom, it has been put forth that the California Air Resources Board (CARB) Chair Liane Randolph will be retiring from state service effective September 30, 2025, and Senior Advisor to the Governor for Climate Lauren Sanchez would serve as the next CARB Chair. U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has issued a supplemental notice of proposed rulemaking (SNPRM) that revises the proposed Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) â€œSet 2â€ renewable volume requirements (RVOs) for 2026 and 2027 to account for the small refinery exemption (SRE) decisions.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_604201" align="alignnone" width="772"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 1.93% WoW decline, falling from $56.61 to $55.52 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices decreased by 1.53% WoW, falling from $56.85 to $55.98. Type 1 trades witnessed 442,613 credits exchanged, reflecting a 213.01% rise WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw an increase of 18.32% WoW, reaching 758,894 credits. [caption id="attachment_604203" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_604202" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 1.80% WoW, reaching $54.63/MT. On 16th September, the spot price stood at $51.63/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $53.75/MT as of 16th September

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_604204" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 55 trades were reported in the market, with 4 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $85.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 15,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $44.50 per credit for one Type 2 transaction reported for 5,000 credits. The largest volume of one transfer involved 140,000 credits for Type 2 transactions priced at $56.00. The smallest volume of transfer involved 21 credits for a Type 1 transaction priced at $55.50.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_604205" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $55.83 in September so far, falling from $56.47 in August. Trade volumes for the month of September stood at 1.40M as of 14th September, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_603670" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 8th â€“ 14th September, total VPT recorded a fall of 1.52% WoW, with a fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week. The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 1.38% WoW. Both D4 and D6 prices fell by 3.96% WoW, while D5 price fell by 4.95% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming datesÂ 

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

  • US Appeals Court Voids Biden-Era Electric Vehicle Fuel Economy Rule: A U.S. appealed court voided a Biden administration-era rule that changed the calculation of the petroleum-equivalent fuel economy of electric vehicles, a victory for 13 Republican state attorneys general who said the rule overstated EVs' efficiency. The 8th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals in St. Louis said the Department of Energy exceeded its authority by deciding to phase out during the 2027 to 2030 model years a "fuel content" factor that artificially inflated EV fuel economy, rather than eliminating that factor. Writing for a three-judge panel, Circuit Judge Duane Benton said the department also failed to provide notice of the range of alternatives it was considering.
  • North Americaâ€™s First Hydrogen-Powered ZEMU Passenger Train to Enter Service Sept. 13 in San Bernardino: The long-awaited passenger service debut of San Bernardino County Transportation Authorityâ€™s (SBCTA) hybrid hydrogen fuel cell-battery electric passenger train, known as ZEMU, will begin Metrolink service on Saturday, Sept. 13. The train will depart the Metrolink San Bernardino Downtown Station in the city of San Bernardino at 10:32 a.m. and serve riders on the nine-mile Arrow Corridor of Metrolinkâ€™s San Bernardino Line throughout the day.The train uses a hybrid hydrogen and battery technology to propel the train and power onboard electrical systems. Water vapor is the only emission generated from the propulsion system, making this a clean transit alternative.
  • US Biofuel Plan Would Reallocate Half or Less of Waived Blending Quotas, Sources Say: The White House is considering a plan that would require large oil refineries to cover around half or less of the biofuel blending requirements recently waived for smaller facilities, according to three sources familiar with the matter.The proposal submitted by the Environmental Protection Agency and now under review by the White House would require large refiners to cover a range of about 50% or less of the 1.1 billion gallons of the renewable fuel exempted last month for small plants, according to the sources. The plan, if adopted, would likely frustrate biofuel producers and farm-state lawmakers who want full restoration of lost demand caused by small refinery exemptions.
  • California in Talks to Pay Hundreds of Millions to Valero to Stave Off Refinery Shutdown: California legislators are considering giving Valero Energy Corp. hundreds of millions of dollars to cover refinery maintenance costs in a bid to prevent the closure of a San Francisco-area fuel plant. Under such a deal, the state would pay Valero to continue operating its Benicia refinery, according to people familiar with the negotiations who asked not to be identified discussing private deliberations. The plant is slated to close by April, the latest in a string of recent California fuel-plant shutdowns. Between $80 million and $200 million of state funds would likely be earmarked for routine maintenance work, although the terms of the arrangement could be subject to change, the people said. Maintenance is one of the biggest operating costs for refiners and the expense of major overhauls typically performed every four or five years can be a catalyst for closure.
  • CARB Chair Liane Randolph to retire from state service: Governor Gavin Newsom announced that California Air Resources Board (CARB) Chair Liane Randolph will be retiring from state service effective September 30, 2025 and named Senior Advisor to the Governor for Climate Lauren Sanchez to serve as the next CARB Chair. During her time as Chair, CARB expanded its focus on improving conditions in communities that suffer from the highest levels of air pollution in the state, and the Board has adopted landmark climate and environmental policies, including the 2022 Scoping Plan laying out Californiaâ€™s path to carbon neutrality by 2045, and implementing Governor Newsomâ€™s 2020 executive order on zero-emission vehicles, accelerating the transition to a cleaner transportation system. Chair Randolph dedicated the majority of her career to public service, including more than 20 years in state leadership roles, most recently as CARB Chair since 2021. Prior to her work at CARB, Randolph served as a Commissioner at the California Public Utilities Commission from 2015 to 2021, Deputy Secretary and General Counsel at the California Natural Resources Agency from 2011 to 2014 and Chair of the California Fair Political Practices Commission from 2003 to 2007.
  • 2025 LCFS Amendment Implementation FAQ: The Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ), related to the implementation of the 2024 LCFS amendments, got updated on 15th September. These FAQs revolve around the areas- CI benchmarks, credit generation, CI exceedance deficit obligation, conservation margin of safety (MOS), sustainability requirements, online feedstock tracking platforms, and other applications of CI benchmark.
  • EPA Proposes Renewable Fuel Standards Supplemental Rule, Addresses Small Refinery Exemptions: S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is issuing a supplemental notice of proposed rulemaking (SNPRM) that revises the proposed Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) â€œSet 2â€ renewable volume requirements (RVOs) for 2026 and 2027 to account for the small refinery exemption (SRE) decisions issued on August 22, 2025. This proposal is an important step in the EPAâ€™s tireless work to repair the damage done after four years of the Biden-Harris Administration neglecting key components of the RFS program, including threats to transform the RFS program into a subsidy for electric vehicle charging stations as part of a larger strategy to electrify the transportation sector.
